task: I’m comparing a couple of fund updates before I send a note to the team, and I need a practical way to keep the review organized so I don’t miss anything important. Can you help me set up a straightforward process for going through them and capturing the main differences?

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — a simple **3-pass review** works well for this and keeps you from getting lost in the noise.

## 1) First pass: hard numbers only
Scan both updates quickly and capture the items that are most likely to matter:

- **NAV / performance**
- **AUM / fund size**
- **Cash / liquidity**
- **Leverage**
- **Redemptions / subscriptions / flows**
- **Covenant changes**
- **Maturity / refinancing / extension changes**
- Any **gating / suspension / side pocket** type items

Goal: identify the facts that changed, not the commentary around them.

## 2) Second pass: explain the change
For anything that moved, ask:

- **What changed?**
- **Why did it change?**
- **Is it one-off or recurring?**
- **Does it affect risk, liquidity, or outlook?**

This is where you separate a meaningful shift from a routine update.

## 3) Third pass: decide what to mention
Classify each item into:

- **Material** — include in your note to the team
- **Watchlist** — worth tracking, but not worth highlighting now
- **Noise** — can ignore

A good test is:  
**Would this change affect how the team thinks about the fund today?**  
If yes, it’s probably material.

---

## Simple comparison grid
You can reuse this each time:

| Category | Fund Update A | Fund Update B | Change | Material / Watchlist / Noise | Notes |
|---|---|---:|---:|---|---|
| NAV / performance |  |  |  |  |  |
| AUM |  |  |  |  |  |
| Cash / liquidity |  |  |  |  |  |
| Leverage |  |  |  |  |  |
| Flows / redemptions |  |  |  |  |  |
| Covenants |  |  |  |  |  |
| Maturity / extensions |  |  |  |  |  |
| Portfolio / concentration |  |  |  |  |  |
| Other notable items |  |  |  |  |  |

---

## A practical workflow
1. **Read both updates once end-to-end**
2. **Fill the grid with only key figures and changes**
3. **Mark anything unusual**
4. **Tag each item as material / watchlist / noise**
5. **Draft the team note only from the material items**

If you want, I can also turn this into a **one-page template** you can copy/paste into Excel or Word for repeated use.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that works. Clean enough to use without turning it into a project.

The 3-pass setup is the right shape — hard numbers first, then why it moved, then whether it’s actually worth flagging. That matches how I’d want to review it anyway.

I’d probably use it like this:

1. **Baseline the two updates**
   - date / period covered
   - fund / share class
   - whether it’s monthly, quarterly, ad hoc

2. **Pull the same buckets every time**
   - performance / NAV
   - AUM
   - cash / liquidity
   - leverage
   - flows / redemptions
   - concentration / exposures
   - covenant / financing changes
   - any gating, suspension, side pocket, waiver, or extension language

3. **Write one line under each bucket**
   - what changed
   - what caused it
   - whether it’s recurring or one-off
   - whether it affects risk or liquidity

4. **Force a label**
   - material
   - watchlist
   - noise

That part matters. If you don’t force the call, you end up with a bunch of half-notes and no real conclusion.

Your grid is fine. I’d maybe add two columns:
- **Source / page reference**
- **My takeaway**

Keeps the note defensible if somebody asks later why you highlighted something.

If you want, I can also give you a tighter version — like a one-page checklist I’d actually use before sending the team note.
